title: Whether we owe attention to the elderly strangers in our buildings
summary: Argue whether residents have a moral or practical obligation to notice and check on elderly neighbours.
topic: Whether we owe attention to the elderly strangers in our buildings
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether people who share a building have an obligation — moral, practical, or both — to pay attention to elderly neighbours they do not know well. Refer to at least one real case or study (e.g. the 2003 Paris heatwave deaths, or the Japanese concept of 'kodawari' isolation). Use a cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: In August 2003, nearly fifteen thousand people died in France during a heatwave…
What the Paris deaths revealed was not a failure of government but a failure of…
Granted, most of us live beside strangers by choice, not…
The question is whether proximity alone creates any kind of…

hintVocab.meaningVi: kiểm tra phúc lợi / thăm nom
được tìm thấy đã qua đời
sự mệt mỏi vì hàng xóm
bị bỏ qua / không được chú ý
một cái gật đầu ở hành lang
không được chú ý / không ai hay biết
mạng lưới an toàn phi chính thức

sampleText:
In August 2003, nearly fifteen thousand people died in France during a ten-day heatwave. The majority were elderly, and a significant number were found dead in their apartments days after the temperature had fallen. Many had been known to their neighbours only by sight — a face in the hallway, a name on a letterbox. What the Paris deaths revealed was not a failure of government alone but a failure of proximity: people were dying within metres of others who did not know they existed.

This is an uncomfortable fact about modern apartment living. We share walls, stairwells, and post boxes with people whose last names we do not know and whose daily routines go entirely unnoticed. For the elderly and those who live alone, this anonymity is not merely inconvenient — it can be fatal. A person who falls in a bathroom, or who stops collecting mail, can go undetected for days in a building full of residents. The question is not whether we find this troubling but whether we are willing to act on the discomfort.

Granted, the case against obligation has genuine force. Most of us live beside strangers by design, and there is something important about the right not to be watched or visited without invitation. Checking in on elderly neighbours can slide into surveillance; a welfare check that is not wanted is an imposition. What's more, the burden of informal care should not fall on residents when it could be met by professional services or community programmes.

Even so, the argument from inconvenience collapses when the alternative is indifference. A nod in the hallway costs nothing; knowing a neighbour's first name takes perhaps two minutes. What is being asked is not heroism but the minimum recognition that we share a physical space with other people who can be harmed by being overlooked. The informal safety net that neighbours provide — a knock on the door after an unusual silence, a note slipped under a letterbox — is not a replacement for social services but a supplement that can mean the difference between discovery and death.

Solidarity, in the end, is not a virtue suited only to people we know. The elderly stranger three floors up is not our responsibility in the legal sense, but the fact of proximity carries its own quiet moral weight. What happened in Paris in 2003 was allowed to happen partly because a building full of people had agreed, without discussion, that noticing was someone else's job.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to name a hidden cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the Paris deaths revealed was not a failure of government alone but a failure of proximity' dùng cấu trúc 'What X revealed was …' để đặt kết luận quan trọng nhất — sự thất bại của sự gần gũi — ở cuối câu, nơi nó nhận được nhấn mạnh lớn nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the Paris deaths revealed was not a failure of government alone but a failure of proximity' uses a what-cleft to hold the key conclusion until the very end of the sentence, maximising its impact.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to foreground the affected person
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'a person who falls in a bathroom … can go undetected for days' — câu này dùng cấu trúc bị động ẩn ('go undetected') để nhấn mạnh vào nạn nhân chứ không phải những người bỏ sót họ, tạo ra hiệu ứng thảm thương hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'can go undetected for days' uses a passive-equivalent construction ('go + past participle') to focus attention on the vulnerable person rather than on the neighbours who missed them.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào tháng Tám năm 2003, gần mười lăm nghìn người chết ở Pháp trong đợt nắng nóng kéo dài mười ngày. Đa số là người cao tuổi, và một số đáng kể được tìm thấy đã qua đời trong căn hộ của họ nhiều ngày sau khi nhiệt độ hạ xuống. Nhiều người chỉ được hàng xóm biết qua ngoại hình — một khuôn mặt ở hành lang, một cái tên trên hộp thư. Điều mà những cái chết ở Paris tiết lộ không chỉ là sự thất bại của chính phủ mà là sự thất bại của sự gần gũi: người ta đang chết ngay trong tầm mét của những người khác không biết họ tồn tại.

Đây là một sự thật khó chịu về cuộc sống chung cư hiện đại. Chúng ta chia sẻ tường, cầu thang và hộp thư với những người mà chúng ta không biết họ tên và lịch trình hàng ngày của họ hoàn toàn không được chú ý. Đối với người cao tuổi và những người sống một mình, sự ẩn danh này không chỉ bất tiện — nó có thể chết người. Một người ngã trong phòng tắm, hay ngừng lấy thư, có thể không được phát hiện trong nhiều ngày trong một tòa nhà đầy cư dân. Câu hỏi không phải là chúng ta có thấy điều này đáng lo ngại không mà là chúng ta có sẵn sàng hành động dựa trên sự khó chịu đó không.

Công nhận, lập luận chống lại nghĩa vụ có sức mạnh thực sự. Hầu hết chúng ta sống bên cạnh người lạ theo thiết kế, và có điều gì đó quan trọng về quyền không bị theo dõi hay viếng thăm mà không có lời mời. Việc hỏi thăm hàng xóm cao tuổi có thể trượt sang giám sát; một cuộc kiểm tra phúc lợi không được chào đón là sự áp đặt. Hơn nữa, gánh nặng của việc chăm sóc phi chính thức không nên đổ lên cư dân khi nó có thể được đáp ứng bởi các dịch vụ chuyên nghiệp hoặc chương trình cộng đồng.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận từ sự bất tiện sụp đổ khi thay thế là sự thờ ơ. Một cái gật đầu ở hành lang không tốn gì; biết tên hàng xóm chỉ mất vài phút. Điều được yêu cầu không phải là anh hùng mà là sự nhận biết tối thiểu rằng chúng ta chia sẻ không gian vật lý với những người khác có thể bị tổn hại vì bị bỏ qua. Mạng lưới an toàn phi chính thức mà hàng xóm cung cấp — một tiếng gõ cửa sau một khoảng im lặng bất thường, một tờ ghi chú nhét dưới cửa — không phải là sự thay thế cho dịch vụ xã hội mà là sự bổ sung có thể tạo ra sự khác biệt giữa việc được phát hiện và cái chết.

Đoàn kết, cuối cùng, không phải là đức hạnh chỉ dành cho những người chúng ta quen biết. Người cao tuổi xa lạ ở ba tầng phía trên không phải là trách nhiệm của chúng ta theo nghĩa pháp lý, nhưng thực tế của sự gần gũi mang theo trọng lượng đạo đức thầm lặng của riêng nó. Điều xảy ra ở Paris năm 2003 được phép xảy ra một phần vì một tòa nhà đầy người đã đồng ý, không cần thảo luận, rằng việc chú ý là việc của người khác.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to front the writer's key finding?
options: What the Paris deaths revealed was not a failure of government alone but a failure of proximity.
The Paris deaths revealed what was a failure not government but proximity.
It was what revealed the Paris deaths that government failed not proximity.
What revealed the Paris deaths was a failure, not government, proximity.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X revealed was Y' delays the key noun (failure of proximity) until the end for maximum emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … revealed was …' with the key idea at the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the most natural concession opener at B2 level?
options: Granted, the case against obligation has genuine force.
Even though granted is the obligation case genuine force.
Of course but not obligation force it is genuine.
Conceding which obligation against case it force genuine.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' admits the strength of the opposing view before pivoting back to the writer's position.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the cleanest, most natural single-word concession opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds the affected person?
options: Neighbours missed a person who fell for days.
A person can go undetected for days in a building full of residents.
A person detected not can go for days residents building.
Days building residents person undetected falls can.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Go undetected' (passive-equivalent) puts the vulnerable person in subject position, not the neighbours who failed to notice.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps the vulnerable person as the grammatical subject?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best adds a further supporting point after mentioning the informal safety net?
options: In conclusion, neighbours are very important.
Throughout history, people have always helped each other.
not a replacement for social services but a supplement that can mean the difference between discovery and death.
It is important to note that neighbours should help.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C stays specific and uses a concrete contrast ('replacement' vs 'supplement') with a striking final image.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option adds a precise, concrete distinction rather than a platitude?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'proximity' mean in this context?
options: Emotional closeness between friends
The legal duty of care between citizens
Physical nearness — sharing the same building or floor
A formal neighbourhood watch arrangement
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Proximity' in this essay refers to physical nearness — living in the same building — which the writer argues creates a mild moral obligation.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the physical, not emotional, meaning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'can slide into' correctly to warn of an unintended consequence?
options: Checking in on elderly neighbours can slide into surveillance.
Checking in elderly neighbours can slides into surveillance.
Checking in on elderly neighbours slides can into surveillance.
Checking in on elderly neighbours can sliding into surveillance.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Can slide into' uses modal + base verb correctly and implies a gradual, unwanted drift.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs are followed by the base (infinitive) form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase is a fixed collocation meaning 'to visit someone informally to see if they are well'?
options: check up a person
check in on someone
check someone about
check out someone
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Check in on someone' is the standard collocation for an informal visit to verify someone's wellbeing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition combination is standard for this meaning?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most effectively avoids AI-style filler in an introduction?
options: In today's modern world, loneliness among the elderly is a very serious problem.
In August 2003, nearly fifteen thousand people died in France during a ten-day heatwave.
It is important to note that elderly people can be isolated.
Throughout history, the elderly have always needed support from their community.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B opens with a specific date, place, number, and event — no generic framing needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Concrete number + date + event beats any generic opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals that the writer is returning to their main position after a concession?
options: On the contrary,
Even so, the argument from inconvenience collapses when the alternative is indifference.
But whatever,
In conclusion,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges the conceded point and pivots cleanly back to the main argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option acknowledges the previous concession and makes a strong pivot?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which metaphor does the essay use for what neighbours informally provide?
options: A civic duty
A welfare check
An informal safety net
A moral weight
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Informal safety net' is the metaphor used to describe the network of small acts (knocking, leaving notes) that can protect isolated elderly people.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a net-related image in the fourth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word in the essay means 'complete lack of concern or interest'?
options: proximity
solidarity
indifference
imposition
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Indifference' means a lack of interest or concern, used in the essay as the outcome the writer most wants to challenge.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word contains the prefix meaning 'not'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause for people?
options: A person who falls in a bathroom can go undetected for days.
A person which falls in a bathroom can go undetected for days.
A person that falls a bathroom can go for days undetected.
A person, who falls in bathroom, can undetected for days go.
mcQuestions.explanation: Defining relative clauses for people use 'who' (or 'that') and no commas. Option A is correct.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'who' for people in defining clauses; no commas.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means the same as 'the burden of informal care should not fall on residents'?
options: Residents should not be expected to provide informal care.
Residents always provide too much informal care.
Informal care should be refused by all residents.
Residents are unable to provide any care.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should not fall on' means 'should not be placed as a responsibility upon'; option A captures this accurately.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option restates 'should not be made responsible for'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps a consistent B2 formal register?
options: Old people die in flats and nobody cares which is sad tbh.
The fact of proximity carries its own quiet moral weight.
Like, elderly people in buildings kinda deserve more attention right?
Old people r alone and neighbours should help them obviously!!
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise vocabulary ('proximity', 'moral weight') and a controlled, reflective tone appropriate for B2.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang, abbreviations, and rhetorical exclamations.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not … but …' for contrast?
options: The question is not whether we find this troubling but whether we are willing to act.
The question is not whether we find this troubling or willing to act.
The question not is whether we find this but we are willing to act.
Not the question whether we find or willing acting on this.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not … but …' creates a precise contrast between two parallel clauses: 'whether we find this troubling' vs 'whether we are willing to act'.
mcQuestions.hint: Both halves of 'not … but …' should be grammatically parallel.

mcQuestions.prompt: What event in France does the essay use as its key example, and why is it effective?
options: A government report on loneliness published in 2003 — because it is an official source
The 2003 heatwave deaths — because it is a specific, real event with concrete numbers and a clear cause
A fictional story about an elderly woman — because fiction is easier to relate to
A general survey of European apartment dwellers — because statistics are always convincing
mcQuestions.explanation: The 2003 heatwave deaths are an effective example because they combine a specific date, location, number (15,000), and a traceable social cause (isolation), making the abstract argument concrete.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what makes an example convincing: specificity, real event, traceable cause.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you know the name of the oldest person on your floor or in your building? If not, what would it take — practically and psychologically — for you to find out?
